title: Quiz: Inner Products and Hilbert Spaces
description: Test your understanding of inner products, orthogonality, and Hilbert spaces

questions.question: What is ⟨0|1⟩ for the computational basis states |0⟩ = (1,0)ᵀ and |1⟩ = (0,1)ᵀ?
questions.explanation: ⟨0|1⟩ = 1*·0 + 0*·1 = 0. The computational basis states are orthogonal.

questions.question: For |u⟩ = (1, i)ᵀ, what is ⟨u|u⟩?
questions.explanation: ⟨u|u⟩ = 1*·1 + (i)*·i = 1 + (-i)(i) = 1 + (-i²) = 1 + 1 = 2. Remember we conjugate the bra (first) vector.

questions.question: If |ψ⟩ is a normalized quantum state, what is ⟨ψ|ψ⟩?
questions.explanation: By definition, a normalized state satisfies ⟨ψ|ψ⟩ = 1. This ensures total probability equals 1.

questions.question: What is the probability of measuring |0⟩ if the system is in state |+⟩ = (|0⟩ + |1⟩)/√2?
questions.explanation: P(|0⟩) = |⟨0|+⟩|² = |1/√2|² = 1/2. Each basis state has equal probability in |+⟩.

questions.question: The inner product satisfies ⟨u|v⟩ = ⟨v|u⟩ (it is symmetric).
questions.explanation: False! The inner product is conjugate-symmetric: ⟨u|v⟩ = ⟨v|u⟩*. The complex conjugate is required.

questions.question: Two orthogonal quantum states can always be perfectly distinguished by a measurement.
questions.explanation: True! Orthogonal states (⟨ψ|φ⟩ = 0) can be perfectly distinguished because measuring in a basis containing both states will never confuse them. The probability of measuring one when the system is in the other is |⟨ψ|φ⟩|² = 0.

questions.question: What does the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality tell us about the inner product of two normalized states?
questions.explanation: Cauchy-Schwarz states |⟨u|v⟩| ≤ ||u||·||v||. For normalized states, both norms equal 1, so |⟨ψ|φ⟩| ≤ 1.

questions.question: Which of the following are properties of a valid inner product? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a), b), and c) are the defining properties of an inner product. d) is false - the inner product is NOT commutative, only conjugate-symmetric.
